items.mistake: Training without aligning X and y row-by-row
items.fix: Ensure every feature row x_i has exactly one corresponding label y_i in the same order; shuffle X and y together, never independently.
items.why: Misaligned pairs teach the model nonsense associations and produce silently wrong results.
items.example: Wrong: shuffle(X) and shuffle(y) separately. Right: idx = np.random.permutation(n); X[idx], y[idx].

items.mistake: Evaluating on the same labeled data used for training
items.fix: Hold out validation and test sets with labels; report metrics only on data the model did not train on.
items.why: Training-set accuracy inflates when the model memorizes labels — it does not measure generalization.
items.example: Train on 80% (X_train, y_train); report accuracy on 20% (X_test, y_test) the model never saw during fit().

items.mistake: Using supervised learning when no reliable labels exist
items.fix: Consider unsupervised, self-supervised, or weak-label strategies; or invest in labeling before committing to supervised training.
items.why: Without trustworthy y, the supervision signal is missing or misleading — the paradigm cannot work as designed.
items.example: 10k product images with no tags: clustering (unsupervised) or contrastive pretraining beats fake random labels.

items.fix: Audit features for post-outcome data (e.g. refund flag when predicting fraud at checkout); remove or time-split leaky columns.
items.why: Label leakage makes training loss artificially low but deployment performance collapses.
items.example: Predicting churn with feature 'account_closed_date' — that field is only set after the customer already left.
